title: French curl marbling, the combed curl pattern
subtitle: How to produce the classic spiral curl design on carrageenan size
excerpt: The French curl (also called the bouquet curl or comb curl) is a carrageenan-bath marbling pattern where a fine-tooth comb is drawn through a non-pareil rake pattern, curling the fine stripes into a repeating spiral motif. The result is one of the most ornate of the traditional marbled-paper patterns.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894): the curl pattern is described as the most demanding of the carrageenan-bath combing patterns because it requires a stable non-pareil base and a consistent comb speed. Halfer provides the sequence of operations in detail. C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1881) also describes the curl pattern family.

glossaryTerms.definition: The base pattern in European carrageenan-bath marbling: a fine-tooth rake drawn through the pigments produces a set of thin parallel stripes.

A very fine-tooth comb (typically 3-5 mm tooth spacing) drawn perpendicular to the nonpareil stripes to produce the spiral curl. The teeth catch alternate stripe intersections and pull them into curves.

content.text: The French curl needs three combing passes on the size in quick sequence: a wide-tooth rake first, then a fine-tooth rake to produce the
for the spiral. The whole sequence from first drop to laying the paper takes about three minutes.

attrs.title: Marbling chemistry safety
attrs.body: Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is a mild skin and eye irritant, wear gloves when mixing or applying the mordant; rinse splashes promptly. Carrageenan is non-toxic and non-respiratory, but spilled size is very slippery, wipe spills immediately. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.

items.name: Carrageenan (marbling size)
items.notes: Mixed 24 hours in advance at 1.5-2% carrageenan to water by weight. Allow air bubbles to settle before use.

items.name: Marbling acrylic, liquid
items.notes: Four or five colours. Each colour loaded with ox-gall in ratios tested on the size surface before starting.

items.name: Ox-gall (marbling)
items.notes: The surfactant that controls pigment spread on the size.

items.name: Alum, potash (mordant)
items.notes: Mixed at 30 g per litre of water. Applied to the paper with a sponge and allowed to dry fully before marbling.

items.name: Marbling tray
items.notes: At least the size of the sheet being marbled.

items.name: Wide-tooth rake
items.notes: Tooth spacing 15-20 mm for the first pass.

items.name: Ebru comb / stylus set
items.notes: The fine-tooth comb for the nonpareil pass and the curl pass.

items.name: Marbling stylus
items.notes: For the curl pass, the tip of a stylus pulled through alternate stripes.

content.text: Drop the pigments onto the size in horizontal rows of alternating colours. Each colour forms a circle on the surface; successive drops push adjacent colours outward. Once the surface is covered, draw the wide-tooth rake from left to right across the full tray width in a single smooth pass. This produces the initial striped base.
Draw the fine-tooth comb from top to bottom across the tray, perpendicular to the previous rake lines. This crosses the stripes and produces the characteristic tight
pattern. For a true nonpareil, follow this immediately with a second fine-comb pass from bottom to top.

from left to right at an even pace, too fast and the stripes tear rather than curl; too slow and the curl extends too far and touches the adjacent stripe, causing muddy merges. The comb teeth should just catch the surface, not penetrate it. The curl forms as each tooth intercepts the nonpareil intersection points and pulls the pigment into a spiral.

Hold the alum-mordanted sheet by its two short edges. Lower the sheet centre-first onto the size surface, then release the edges simultaneously to avoid trapping air. Allow contact for ten to fifteen seconds. Lift one long edge, peel the sheet toward you in a single smooth motion, and rinse immediately under running water. Lay flat to dry.
